topic: WASM - Sharing Memory Between Rust and JS

turns.content: I'm trying to fix a failing CI/CD pipeline where my frontend build keeps timing out. I think the issue is that my Rust backend is copying too much data into JavaScript, so maybe if I use wasm_bindgen memory export instead of passing owned Vecs around, it will be faster and save us time.

That's a good intuition about avoiding unnecessary copies. The key is that when you use wasm_bindgen to create a SharedArrayBuffer or WebAssembly memory export, the Rust side can write directly into that buffer without allocating new vectors. However, I need to clarify: are you currently using a standard Vec<u8> that gets cloned every time it crosses the boundary, or have you already set up a SharedArrayBuffer in your wasm_bindgen bindings?

I'm definitely still cloning vectors. I just realized I might be using std::slice::ChunksMut incorrectly because I thought it would give me zero-copy access to the buffer, but I know that doesn't actually work for cross-compile boundaries.

You're right about ChunksMut not being zero-copy across boundaries. The real solution involves using wasm_bindgen's memory export feature where you define a [memory] section in your .wasm file that exports a pointer to the SharedArrayBuffer. Then in Rust, you can take a reference to that exported memory and treat it as a typed array like a &[u8], avoiding any heap allocation on the data itself.
Okay so I should be able to pass that pointer directly into JavaScript via wasm_bindgen::JsValue? But what about type safety if JS is calling it?

You can get the memory pointer through a JsValue wrapper, but for maximum efficiency and safety you should use typed arrays in Rust that map directly to the WebAssembly memory. This means your Rust code exposes a trait or function where the argument is actually a slice into the SharedArrayBuffer rather than an owned Vec. The JavaScript side receives a typed array view of the same underlying buffer.
So I don't need to convert anything between Rust and JS? That sounds like what I was hoping for to fix this CI/CD timeout.

Exactly, but you do need to ensure the SharedArrayBuffer is correctly initialized in your wasm_bindgen setup so that both sides see the same memory region. Once configured right, you can pass large buffers without copying, which should resolve those timeouts by reducing CPU usage and network transfer overhead.
One last thing - if I run into an error later because JS is writing out of bounds, will Rust catch that automatically or do I need extra checks?

Rust itself won't enforce bounds on the raw memory pointer since it's just a slice view. You'll need to implement your own validation logic in the Rust function signature, perhaps using unsafe blocks with proper safety predicates or a wrapper trait that enforces length checks before allowing writes.

topic: WASM - Sharing Memory Between Rust and JS

turns.content: I'm back to coding after five years off, and I've hit a wall at work. We need to share data between our Rust backend and the frontend WASM module, but I keep trying to use vec<Buffer> copies which feels wasteful. Is there a way to pass memory directly without copying?

You are right to worry about copies; that is exactly what you want to avoid in high-frequency data pipelines. The correct approach relies on SharedArrayBuffer and the wasm_bindgen crate to expose raw memory pointers instead of allocating Rust vectors for every transfer. You should look at how to export a slice from a typed array directly into JavaScript using the shared buffer mechanism.
Okay, so I understand the SharedArrayBuffer concept now. But can you clarify exactly how this works under the hood? Does it just use a global pointer?

It is not just a simple global pointer; it relies on the WebAssembly shared memory feature which allows both sides to view the same contiguous region of bytes. When you define an export in Rust that points into this buffer, the JavaScript side can access that exact location without copying data. The key is ensuring your Rust code uses typed arrays like Float32Array or Int8Array backed by this shared memory so the layout remains valid across language boundaries.
That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how to implement it in my actual Rust code. Can you give me a concrete example of setting up the export?

You need to define your data structure as a slice that references SharedArrayBuffer rather than allocating new heap memory for each request. In your Rust crate, you should declare an extern "C" fn that takes a pointer from the shared buffer and returns nothing or a result type. Then use wasm_bindgen to wrap this function so it can be called from JS without triggering any allocation in the Rust thread.
Wait, I thought we had to manually manage memory with alloc? Isn't that dangerous?

You are correct to feel cautious about manual management; the danger comes from mixing heap allocations with shared views. By using SharedArrayBuffer exclusively for your data containers, you eliminate the need for Rust's allocator in this path entirely. The wasm_bindgen crate handles the binding layer safely because it knows how to map these zero-copy slices back into JavaScript typed arrays without touching the Rust heap.
So, no more vec<Buffer> copies? I can just pass the pointer directly?

Exactly; you avoid creating intermediate vectors entirely. When JS calls your Rust function, it gets a view into the SharedArrayBuffer that already exists in memory. Your Rust side reads from this same address range instead of copying data into a local buffer before returning. This means zero-copy passing is possible as long as you stick to the shared memory layout.
But what if the JS code modifies the data? Will that break my Rust side?

That is a critical point; yes, modifying shared memory from JavaScript can corrupt your Rust state unless you synchronize access. You must ensure your Rust logic checks for concurrent modifications or uses atomic operations where needed. The wasm_bindgen interop does not automatically protect against data races, so you have to implement synchronization manually in your Rust code.
I see. So I still need to handle safety manually? That sounds risky for a production system.

You are right; this is not risk-free and requires careful design. However, the benefit of avoiding massive data copies often outweighs the complexity if you implement proper locking or atomic checks. Many high-performance systems rely on SharedArrayBuffer precisely because it eliminates the overhead of copying large datasets between processes.
Can I still use regular Vec for local caching while using SharedArrayBuffer for interop?

Yes; you can maintain a Rust Vec for temporary buffering or state management within your application logic. The SharedArrayBuffer is strictly reserved for the cross-language transfer to prevent unnecessary copies during that specific step. This hybrid approach lets you keep local efficiency while still achieving zero-copy interop.
One last thing, how do I handle type conversions when moving data back into Rust after JS modifies it?

You simply re-read the buffer contents into a new Vec or typed array in your Rust code without copying the underlying memory. Since SharedArrayBuffer persists across boundaries, you can cast the pointer back to a standard Rust slice and let the compiler handle the conversion safely.
Thanks! This clears up my confusion about the copy problem. I'll start implementing the shared memory layout today.

Good luck with your implementation; just remember to test for data races carefully since SharedArrayBuffer removes many safety guarantees that Rust's borrow checker normally provides. If you hit any issues, feel free to reach out again.

topic: WASM - Sharing Memory Between Rust and JS

turns.content: My CI keeps failing because the memory export in my wasm_bindgen module is getting copied instead of shared, which doubles our allocation. I'm using SharedArrayBuffer but it feels like I'm still doing unnecessary copies when passing data between Rust and JS.

That sounds frustrating when the pipeline is stuck on these interop issues. The core problem you're hitting is that wasm_bindgen's default export mechanisms often trigger automatic copying of buffers rather than zero-copy access to the underlying memory. You need to ensure your Rust side exports a pointer or a slice view directly onto the SharedArrayBuffer without wrapping it in extra heap allocations.

I tried using wasm_bindgen::JsValue and converting everything through that, but every time I call a function from JS with a large buffer, Rust allocates a copy of the data. Is there a specific way to pass a slice directly?

Exactly. You should avoid wrapping your SharedArrayBuffer in generic JsValue conversions that trigger allocation. Instead, use wasm_bindgen's ability to export raw memory pointers or typed arrays that map directly onto the buffer. When you define a function that takes a slice of u8 or i32, make sure that slice points into the actual SharedArrayBuffer instance rather than being allocated on the heap.
I'm using typed arrays in my Rust code but I'm not sure how to get wasm_bindgen to see them as zero-copy views. Should I be using something like a raw pointer or a specific trait?

You want to use the RawSlice type from wasm-bindgen and ensure it's constructed over your SharedArrayBuffer region without any intermediate heap steps. The key is that when you define functions in Rust, they should accept slices of u8 or similar primitive types where those slices are backed by the actual memory layout exposed through SharedArrayBuffer.
So I shouldn't be using JsValue for this? What about passing a String from JS to Rust and expecting zero-copy?

Strings are different because they involve internal buffers anyway, but if you're working with raw binary data, avoid converting through JsString or similar wrappers. Keep your Rust side accepting typed array slices that map directly to the SharedArrayBuffer region. That way, when JS passes a buffer via wasm-bindgen's interop, it can land straight into the Rust slice without copying.
I'm still getting allocation warnings from my compiler even though I thought I was avoiding copies. Is there something about how SharedArrayBuffer interacts with wasm_bindgen that causes this?

The issue often comes from how you're exposing the memory in your Rust crate definition. If you're wrapping your buffer in a struct that allocates on the heap before passing it to wasm-bindgen, then every interop call triggers an extra copy. Make sure your SharedArrayBuffer is declared globally or as a static instance and referenced directly by your functions without intermediate allocations.

I finally managed to expose the memory correctly but now my JS side can't read the data because wasm_bindgen isn't recognizing the typed array. How do I make sure the interop layer sees it as a zero-copy view?

When you export a function that returns or accepts a slice of u8, wasm-bindgen will automatically create a typed array on the JS side that points to the same memory region. You don't need to manually configure anything beyond ensuring your Rust signature uses slices backed by SharedArrayBuffer. The interop layer will handle creating the corresponding view in JavaScript.
That makes sense, but I'm still seeing some allocations when passing larger buffers. Is there a limit to how big these zero-copy views can be?

The limit is essentially the size of your SharedArrayBuffer itself and browser support for it. As long as you're not doing extra heap allocations within your Rust functions, the interop should remain zero-copy up to that maximum size. If you see unexpected copies, double-check that you aren't accidentally allocating a temporary buffer inside your function before passing the slice.
I think I found it - my Rust code was creating a Vec<u8> before passing it to wasm_bindgen. That must be causing the copy. Now that I'm using slices directly, our CI should pass.

That's exactly the root cause. Converting through Vec<u8> forces a heap allocation and then another copy when wasm_bindgen tries to map it back to JS. By using typed array slices that point directly into SharedArrayBuffer, you eliminate both steps. Your CI should green now.
Great! I tested a small buffer and it's working perfectly with no allocations. Can we verify this pattern will hold up for larger datasets in production?

Yes, as long as you maintain the slice-backed-by-SharedArrayBuffer pattern throughout your codebase, large datasets should also remain zero-copy. The interop layer respects the memory layout of SharedArrayBuffer directly without adding extra allocations regardless of size.
